My point of view, if it can help:

- I don't think numerical value for verbosity level is the way to go in 2015

- Having fine grained on what header and/or body you want from request and/or response with a few options such as `verbose.request.body: true` sounds sane.

- Handling file redirection looks like a rabbit hole you don't want to go into. Let Gabbi have a neat general output that is parseable so anyone can redirect all Gabbi output to a file and then parse and split it as they prefer

- You can try to detect if the body is binary and omit or limit the output

- You can anyway by default limit the ouput to N chars for bodies with an option to control that length, in case someone would need it obviously

This library is a port of an ID generation strategy that was invented in NodeJS. Your question is essentially *Why generate the ID this way, when you can do it simpler this way?"

I would be careful about considering using the standard Math library random() function as the basis for a unique ID. I am not a crypto specialist or anything, but I have read enough articles about the theory to know that the standard random() functions in most languages do not generate a high degree of randomness. For a *table* id, it probably is good enough, but I get the feeling that some of the techniques used in ShortID are to support better randomness as well as improved randomness within clusters.

If you want to have the author of this strategy explain why it works the way it does and how it compares to just using random() with base64 encoding, I would ask dylang.

A bundle as a package of PROV records and an entity "pointing" to that package are two different concepts. The former can be seen as an RDF named graph while the latter a resource representing that named graph in other triples.

I believe the sentence you quoted should be interpreted that if you refer to a bundle as a resource, it can be inferred to be a PROV entity.

Going back to your the example you copied, it is valid to say `wasAttributedTo(a:book, a:author)` without having to say anything about `a:book`. In the example, you assert the provenance of a bundle entity, and the fact that that entity is not described in the document does not make that statement invalid.

From [PROV typing contraint](https://www.w3.org/TR/prov-constraints/#typing), we can infer that `a:book` is an entity. This is similar to RDF type inferences. Your application will need to decide whether you want such inferences to be made. The prov library, however, should not automatically make that decision, I think.

(BTW, I think PROV-CONSTRAINTS misses a typing inference for bundle entities as you found in the PROV-DM specs!)

And a description of the values Slab, SReclaimable, and SUnreclaim: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/406773

It seems that SReclaimable is that memory that could easily be used by other processes if necessary while SUnreclaim cannot be. SReclaimable + SUnreclaim = Slab. 

On this particular machine with ~377.7 GiB of RAM, SUnreclaim is 170.5 MiB while SReclaimable is 180.5 GiB.

So in the end, I believe that if `SReclaimable` is in /proc/meminfo, it needs to be added to `cached`. If only `Slab` (but not SReclaimable or SUnreclaim) it should probably be added to `cached` as well. This will allow the `used` data to be much MUCH more useful, as on high-memory machines this 50% chunk of total memory is quite a lot...

The behavior is that stdin.read() never returns. To the user, the rebase
hangs.

 It is independent of Python version (and language-independent too); it's a
well-known pitfall of the posix 2-stream IO design.

I'm on a phone right now, but check the docs (or implementation!) for
subprocess.communicate, which was written to avoid the deadlock. Should
have pointers to a full explanation.

In a nutshell: pipe buffers are limited in size. the writer is waiting on
the reader to drain stderr before it can write more. But the reader is
waiting on the writer to write all of stdout before reading any stderr.

Technically the everyone role mention is different from the everyone mention. Since the everyone role is not mentionable (and cannot be), the former will never mention anyone. The `@@everyone` is actually `<@&default_role_id>`, same as the other role mentions. 

This creates a situation where role.mentionable is false, but role.mention will mention the role (everyone), which is perhaps odd.

Almost all of the performance stuff is handled by multiprocessing.pool, which is a great library. The processes that pool spins up require very little memory on their own, but if a lot of data is being passed to the concurrent function there's a potential for a lot of memory being used to copy the function arguments into each process's address space. This is sort of a general problem in parallel programming with Python. Because we're forced to use separate processes, any required data needs to be copied around to all of them.

However these processes do get periodically destroyed, specifically after every synchronization event. This means if you use `@synchronized`, the processes are guaranteed to be closed before you leave the decorated function. Otherwise they get closed after each call to wait().

Just reading 4.1:

Indentation: Since YAML works like Python here, it's definitely possible to do that with the tokenizer.
Context: I don't really understand that all the way. But if it's just how newlines are handled, that should be possible.
Chomping: That's also possible with the tokenizer. Just one or two ifs.

There's also the question if you need to be able to handle all of that in the first version of such a parser.

For a tokenizer we could e.g. use the pygments YAML tokenizer and modify it to make it work with parso:
https://bitbucket.org/birkenfeld/pygments-main/src/7941677dc77d4f2bf0bbd6140ade85a9454b8b80/pygments/lexers/data.py?at=default&fileviewer=file-view-default#data.py-33

Here's a haskell token list: https://hackage.haskell.org/package/YamlReference-0.10.0/docs/Text-Yaml-Reference.html

The grammar stuff is not so clear. It also depends how we handle indents and dedents. 

A scraped grammar (complete):
https://gist.github.com/tociyuki/3936873

A grammar (incomplete)
https://github.com/yaml/Yaml.NET/blob/master/doc/BNF%20Grammar.txt

Another grammar that probably makes most sense:
https://github.com/tzlaine/yaml/blob/master/doc/specs/yaml-LL1.txt

That's just my short research.

Hm. I'm having trouble testing this. My python somehow seems to choke on the type hinting, which is a bit weird. I've thought about it for a while, though, so here's a writeup.

As far as I can tell, it's a good start, but not quite right yet. Best would be if the fetch was super transparent to users. Ideally, what I think should happen is:

1) if there is no link header, the parsed json is returned as is. This seems easy to do.
2) if there IS a link header, a response object is returned. it iterates not over responses, but over the _list_ that is the top-level of the response object and, when it reaches the end of the list, fetches more data.
3) fetch_all et cetera can remain as is, of course, as can the function to get the actual response (there should probably also be an explicit method to get the next page response object)

To not break expectations and probably existing code, the interface should probably also be list-like rather than iterator-like (i.e. allow random access), which would require bidirectional iteration (it's probably fine not to cache anything, though, and just document that certain access patterns are a bad idea).

With all that, I think it also makes sense for the response object to be in its own file, as the streaming code is.

Anybody see any glaring issues with this?
Thanks!

Let me just touch on the spaces issue. You can control the spaces (or even change the `:`/`,` characters) via `separators` kwarg. For example, with `jsonplus`:
```
>>> import jsonplus
>>> d = {'a':1, 'b':2}
>>> jsonplus.dumps(d)
'{"a":1,"b":2}'
>>> jsonplus.dumps(d, separators=(', ', ': '))
'{"a": 1, "b": 2}'
>>> jsonplus.dumps(d, separators=(',', ':'))
'{"a":1,"b":2}'
>>> jsonplus.pretty(d)
'{\n    "a": 1,\n    "b": 2\n}'
>>> print(jsonplus.pretty(d))
{
    "a": 1,
    "b": 2
}
```

Default `separators=(',', ':')` produce compact representation, and they are defined in [`_encoder_default_args`](https://github.com/randomir/jsonplus/blob/master/python/jsonplus/__init__.py#L265). They can be overridden on each `dumps` call. [`pretty`](https://github.com/randomir/jsonplus/blob/master/python/jsonplus/__init__.py#L307) does that.

`simplejson` uses different default separators:
```
>>> simplejson.dumps(d)
'{"a": 1, "b": 2}'
>>> jsonplus.dumps(d)
'{"a":1,"b":2}'
```
but you can change them in the same way as above, via `separators` kwarg.

Regarding the `__init__.py` size -- completely agree, I have that refactor in mind. :)

Cheers!

OperationalError: Exception raised for errors that are related to the
database's operation and not necessarily under the control of the
programmer, e.g. an unexpected disconnect occurs ...

Given that the API assumes that some connection errors will raise this
exception it makes the most sense to me to add a timeout
exception/connection attempt failed exception as a sub.

That said, I can see an argument for subbing InterfaceError as well:

InterfaceError:  Exception raised for errors that are related to the
database interface rather than the database itself...

I may be missing the point. But just in case I am not.

  - An indicator uses data input to generate data output

Even if `backtrader` offers facilities to avoid the user having to iterate over the data, the data has to be iterated over.

Looking at your definition:
```
def __init__(self):
    results = pd.ols(y=self.data.close,x=self.data.open,window=10)
```

`self.data.close` at this point has to be considered *empty*. Of course the platform may have preloaded the data as an optimization to speed up things, but the implementation of an indicator has to consider that the data is empty.

> Because the data could come from any source. For example a database which is queried for each data or from a live feed

The next line:
```
self.lines.coeffient = results.beta['x']
```

This is for sure not going to work. Here you assign a (I guess) standard array to a `line`. The `line` doesn't like arrays, it likes other `line` objects, from which it can read the data when it is produced.

But the real point is the one before this assignment as commented before:

  - You want to calculate the regression of `close` prices on `open` prices when they have not even started to exist.

Your best bet is something like

```python
import backtrader as bt
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
class TestIndicator_regress(bt.Indicator):
    lines = ('coeffient',)
    params = (('period',10),)

    def next(self):
        results = pd.ols(
            y=self.data.close.get(size=self.p.period),
            x=self.data.open.get(size=self.p.period),
            window=self.p.period)
        # ATTENTION: I don't know what's in results.beta['x']
        # and self.lines.coefficient[0] expects something that can be converted to a float
        self.lines.coefficient[0] = results.beta['x']
```

Hi,

Thanks for the detailed report.

XlsxWriter doesn't prepend a '='. Excel does that in the interface.

The issue is that the sting `X` needs to be quoted as an Excel string `"X"`, like this:

```python
worksheet.write('A1', '"X"')
```

After that the sample program should work as expected.

![aa_image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/94267/43843739-20d41050-9b20-11e8-8173-eff34150df17.png)



I'll leave this open as an issue (maybe with a changed title) since the module should probably fix this silently in the background.

Thanks for the input.

John

This is a feature that I would very much like to implement but as the ultimate maintainer it is non-trivial to define a consistent and usable API, to plumb the infrastructure and  write all the tests. It would also make adding/maintaining other formatting features harder (for example how would it work with Tables or Conditional Formatting). And a least some of that work would need to be duplicated across the Perl and C versions of the module. Also, it is something that people manage to workaround in different ways so it isn't a blocking feature. As such I haven't go around to doing it. 

There is also a slightly higher gravitational pull on my time to implement features that people can't work around and need code that implements new Excel features. For example, although this is the feature request with the most +1s the feature that I get most separate requests about is additional control over chart data labels! (Frequently requested features are always strange: the most common feature request that I used to get, until I implemented them, was for line breaks).

At the moment I'm also spending my copious free time(tm) trying to get the C version of the module somewhat more feature compatible with the Python version. Check it out if you are interested: [libxlsxwriter](https://github.com/jmcnamara/libxlsxwriter).

Anyway, while waiting for me to circle back around to this feature I think that it would be a good candidate for someone to spin off into a wrapper module and release on PyPI. Something that caches all the `write` and `format` calls and writes them out on `close()`. That was basically my plan anyway, just not as an external module.

@ncaq I read [GNU/Linuxにおけるゴミ箱ディレクトリは.Trash/$uid/と.Trash-$uid/のどちらが正しいのか調べていたらtrash-cliのコメントが動作と違ったのでissueを開きました - ncaq](https://www.ncaq.net/2017/10/15/).

>I read Trash specification
I thought `$topdir/.Trash/$uid` is more compliant with `$topdir/.Trash-$uid`

Trash specification says that when the implementation trash files from the rest of the file system (device/partition), that has “home trash” directory, because of copy cost, the implementation MAY use `$topdir/.Trash/$uid` and `$topdir/.Trash-$uid`.

>https://specifications.freedesktop.org/trash-spec/trashspec-latest.html
>The following paragraph applies ONLY to the case when the implementation supports trashing in the top directory, and a $topdir/.Trash does not exist or has not passed the checks:

**this description is important** to resolve behavior, I think. 

1. Creating `$topdir/.Trash` is only permitted for an administrator(the “sticky bit” in the permissions must be set).
2.  All user MUST have write access partition to `$topdir/.Trash`.
3. When `$topdir/.Trash` exist and pass all checks(“sticky bit”, not a symbolic link, etc. watch Trash specification),
    1. A user can create `$topdir/.Trash/$uid`(the implementation MUST immediately create it).
    2. A user can use `$topdir/.Trash/$uid` as trash directory
4. When `$topdir/.Trash` **doesn't exist** or fail the checks,
    1. A user can create `$topdir/.Trash-$uid`(the implementation MUST immediately create it)
    2. A user can use `$topdir/.Trash-$uid` as trash directory

As far as I know, your investigation is the case when `$topdir/.Trash` is not created by administrator. In this case, the behavior that the implementation use `$topdir/.Trash-$uid` is correct.

Hate to pull the old '+1', but just ran in to this trying to make my lib work with both py2 and py3. Many things seem to be a lot easier than with the builtin `csv` module, but delimiter (and lineterminator) arguments are required to be `str`. My current issue is with the *writer*, not the reader, but I'm assuming this is the same issue at its core. 

As I was already using `six`, my current workaround looks like this:

~~~~python
from __future__ import unicode_literals
import csv
import six

if six.PY2:
    import unicodecsv as csv

def export(fname, …, delimiter='\t', …):
    if six.PY2:
        output = open(fname, 'wb')
        delimiter = delimiter.encode('utf-8')
    else:
        output = open(fname, 'w')
    …
    with output:
        writer = csv.DictWriter(output, …, delimiter=delimiter, …)
        …
~~~~

Getting rid of the first bit would be awesome.

Here a exemple with a jasig CAS as backend. Only parameters set in settings.py are `LOGIN` ([to the file](https://django-cas-server.readthedocs.io/en/latest/README.html#logs) /tmp/cas_server.log) and [`CAS_FEDERATE=True`](https://django-cas-server.readthedocs.io/en/latest/README.html#federation-mode). The CAS provider was added using django admin.

1. select the provider and click Connect

@sdalezman I'm wondering if ignoring ids would actually be a good idea or not myself. I totally understand the reasons to ignore them, but at the same time, why not creating the db entries with a predefined id? In Django for example you can create models with a given id. Ids can also be useful to make assertions on the returned data, and so maybe they should be part of what's tested in the snapshot?
Same thing for dates, like creation timestamps. In Django, using auto_now_add is already considered bad practice as you don't have a way to override that in testing. Why not create fixtures with deterministic, pre-defined dates too?

backports.csv is not intended as a drop-in replacement for Python 2's csv module. It really can't be, because they have fundamentally different modes of working. It is, however, intended to be a complete backport of the paradigm given by the Python 3 csv module.

The reason that backports.csv can't be both things is the same reason that bytes and str were separated in Python 3: they are fundamentally different, and attempting to conflate them makes for sometimes subtle and hard-to-debug errors, because the boundaries between bytes and text are not made clear.

`unicodecsv` is a package that _does_ attempt to be a drop-in replacement for the Python 2 csv module, while adding in unicode support, and it does a reasonably good job of it, with some caveats like that the separators _cannot_ be unicode and there are some encodings that it has trouble with because of the underlying csv implementation (UTF-16, in particular, has null bytes, and totally breaks the parser).

So yes, when you're working with a Python 3 backport library, you _must_ use the correct type. That's what Python 3 requires of you, and it would be doing you a disservice to hide those things that Python 3 requires, simply because you're running on Python 2. I definitely recommend using `from __future__ import unicode_literals` so that you can avoid that pesky `u''` prefix, but that can be brought to a matter of taste.

Yeah, the statement you quoted could be read that way but it doesn't actually say that `use_virtual == True` also means `interactive == False` ;) So the API currently doesn't provide a way to ask *only* whether to open a virtual port.

TBH, I initially only provided the functions in `midiutil` as an easy means to write short test scripts, expecting developers to implement their own functionality for selecting MIDI ports, based on the "official" API (`rtmidi.MidiBase.get_ports()`) for the user interface they need (e.g. within a GUI framework).

I could expand the range of values for the `use_virtual` parameter in the way you suggest, but I'm thinking whether it would not be better, to allow the developer to pass a function, which handles the prompting. This would be passed the port and the `use_virtual` value and would make it easier to integrate the `open_midiinput` / `open_midioutput` functions into different (user interface) frameworks.

I really want to make TranslationField not nullable for my CharField variables, there seems to be no easy way to configure TranslationField.null=False.

A go-around solution could not be easily found on the web, and I had to come up a solution of my own. For anyone who is suffering from any similar problem, below is my temporary solution to this issue. Please remove this post if not proper for this page.

Few points I want to mention with the solution below,
- Model(e.g. Place)'s 'default' value need to be set explicitly to force default value for translated fields
- override_translation_field_nullability function not necessary if you want to simply force null=False for your fields.
- original post for possible future updates: [http://yerihyo.wikidot.com/blog:85](url)

models.py
```python
from django.db import models

class Place(models.Model):
   name = models.CharField(max_length=255, default="",)
```

translation.py
```python
from modeltranslation.translator import translator, TranslationOptions, register
from .models import Place

def override_translation_field_nullability(self, field, translation_field,):
    from django.db.models import fields
    field_instance = self.model._meta.get_field(field)
    
    if isinstance(field_instance,fields.CharField):
        translation_field.null = False

class PlaceTranslationOptions(TranslationOptions):
    fields = ('name',)
    
    def add_translation_field(self, field, translation_field):
        override_translation_field_nullability(self,field,translation_field)
        rv = super(PlaceTranslationOptions,self).add_translation_field(field, translation_field)
        return rv

    
translator.register(Place, PlaceTranslationOptions,)
```

I suppose we could hide different 'backends' behind a single interface, and then people could opt-in to a faster version by setting an alternative backend, if they have the required optional dependencies installed?

I dont have a clear intuition if that would fit well together, or if the different backends would impose subtly different interface requirements... for instance, the indirect sort is something that is often reusable; if you want to compute multiple independent reductions or operations on some indexed set; that is the main motivation for numpy-indexed OOP design. Regarding running tests, web2py has several imports in a try-catch statement and several contrib modules, as a matter of fact what is really missing in the current matrix are tests against all possible imported modules. 
Given the following:
```
try:
    import module_a
except:
    from .contrib import module_a
```
In this case, we should run two tests, the one using module_a and then one using the contrib version.
By doing that, we can guarantee that crontib modules shipped with web2py are updated and inline with the pip version. As as happened recently for portalocker, they are not always inline.
I don't want to run an infinitive combination of the possible modules, just a few for the most important ones.

I've just spent a fair bit of time getting this working in AWS Lambda, so I'm updating here for future reference.

I encountered the same error from thrift_sasl: "Could not start SASL: Error in sasl_client_start (-4) SASL(-4): no mechanism available: No worthy mechs found"

We do not have to statically link these libraries. We need to make sure the shared objects are packaged with our Lambda function, so copy both libsasl2.so and the contents of /usr/lib64/sasl2/ to a folder named 'lib' in the root of your Lambda function package. LD_LIBRARY_PATH is set in Lambda to look in this location when the function runs. 

libsasl2.so will still try to look for libraries in the /usr/lib64/sasl2 location. In order to have it look in the lib folder where our Lambda function exists, we need to set SAML_PATH. This can be done at the top of your function code before importing thrift_sasl (or importing the dependent module):

```
  import os
  os.environ['SASL_PATH'] = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), 'lib')
  from pyhive import hive
```

Note: make sure you are copying the libraries from the same ami that Lambda uses: http://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/current-supported-versions.html

We have 3 concrete examples where it's useful

1. We're trying to get online marketing data from an extremely rate-limited api. Every day we need to re-run the last 4 weeks, but the api breaks when we run it one day at a time. By batching the jobs we can make a single api request with a larger date range and group the results for 28 writes. This uses the range aggregation.
2. We do daily and hourly sqoops of mysql tables into the data warehouse, overwriting the stored version completely. When these tasks get backed up, we only need to run the job with the largest time parameter. By batching these jobs together, we avoid re-sqooping the same table or waiting for all of them to get completed. Note this is basically the same use case as #570. This uses the max aggregation.
3. After doing impala/hive inserts, we need to refresh the table to pick up the changes. Often the scheduler will do a series of inserts into the same table, followed by a series of refreshes of that table. Really, we only need to refresh that table once and every subsequent refresh without another insert is just wasted time and resources. This uses the csv aggregation.

So the assumption that case is ignored has been in the boolean type since it was introduced. The line was blurred when this "Case is ignored" docstring was removed in https://github.com/Pylons/colander/commit/94ca41f3b6b76359295eba1548cafb3ac9e42f76.

I **think** that the assumption that case is ignored should be retained and that the docstring should be made more clear. Robot sets `${EXECDIR}` based on the cwd when test execution starts, but libraries or other code can use [os.chdir](https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.html#os.chdir) to change it.

Perhaps a note could be added that possible relative paths are considered to be relative to the current working directory. Don't think it makes sense to mention `${EXECDIR}`.

There can be multiple reasons why the automation don't work for you and most likely you are facing multiple problems, instead of a one problem. I don't have a solution for your problems, because that would need access to your app and time to investigate the problem. But I have faced similar problems in my automation projects and I can tell the most common problems which I have faced.

1) The automatic test is too fast. Although the element is in the page, it's not ready to receive the interaction. There might be dynamic content, which is not ready and one needs to pool that before, one interacts with the element. And if interaction is done too early, nothing happens in the application side.

2) Stale element reference exception, is same as to the above mentioned problem, but it manifest in different way. Again the key is in polling.

3) Sleeping or using Selenium2Library set selenium speed. Sleeping doesn't help, it can actually make things worse. Replace the sleeping with polling and the test will be faster and rate of false alarms is smaller.

**Event flexibility**: I can definitely put together a button framework for Magicbot. It's certainly needed.

Regarding stopping actions, this isn't explicitly called out in the documentation (though, I will definitely need to add it), but a key part of my philosophy with robot code is this: _During a robot control loop iteration, if a component is not commanded to do something, it should do **nothing** (or a sensible default action)_.

When dealing with components, this is something that one needs to build in yourself. You can see this in the [Shooter component](http://robotpy-wpilib-utilities.readthedocs.io/en/latest/magicbot.html#low-level-components) in the documentation: at the end of the 'execute' function, `self.enabled` is set to False, thus disabling the component during the next iteration _unless something tells it otherwise_. 

However, state machines have this idea explicitly built into them -- the state machine will only execute if someone calls `engage` during the control loop iteration*, else `done` will be called and it will cease execution.

In my opinion, this pattern makes it significantly easier to reason about the behavior of components. If you didn't just tell it to do something, then it isn't doing it (and this property makes it easier to find the component that is causing it *if* it's doing something). I think it's more problematic for runaway components to continue to do something even if nobody is telling it to do so.

_*On occasion it's useful to continue doing something until it is completed, which is why the state machine decorators support the 'must_finish' parameter, which will cause a particular state to continue executing until something causes it to exit that state. It's less problematic for a state machine to have potentially infinite execution, as the ordering of actions is usually well defined and easy to reason about and identify._

**Dashboard feedback**: Currently the information is lost on shutdown. If we upgrade pynetworktables to NT3 (tentatively planned, but it's a lot of work), then one can take advantage of persistent variables and the like... though, I've not explored that much.

### Other comments

**Concurrency**: Ok, so looking at this particular example, I see your point:

```python
group.addParallel(self.move_arm_cmd)
group.addParallel(self.rotate_robot_cmd)
group.addParallel(self.spin_shooter_wheel_cmd)
````
Vs.
```python
class MetaShooter(StateMachine):

    @state
    def wait_for_shoot(self):
        if self.arm.in_position() and self.robot.at_position() and \
           self.shooter.at_speed():
            self.next_state('do_the_shoot')
        
    @state
    def do_the_shoot()
        self.shooter.fire()
```
I think this speaks to the core difference between command-based and how I think about Magicbot then -- in command-based, sequences of actions are very important; whereas for Magicbot I think of it more about "do things when a component is ready", as opposed to "when a sequence is completed". I'm not sure I would go as far as to say one is more correct than the other though.

This also speaks to the infancy of Magicbot -- I could definitely see a meta-component that could implement scheduling like the command group. Maybe?

```python
class MetaShooter(Group):

    def init(self):
        self.add(self.arm.engage,
                 self.robot.rotate,
                 self.shooter.spin_up)
            .until(self.arm.in_position,
                   self.robot.at_position,
                   self.shooter.at_speed)
        self.add(self.shooter.fire)
```
That's still a bit wordy, needs some work. 

**Conflict resolution**: By (a) separating actions from execution (only telling motors to do things in the execute function) and (b) ensuring that a motor/etc is only directly controlled by a single component, a user can effectively ensure that they won't have conflicts. If a user doesn't abide by that... then what can we do?

Lower level components that interact directly with motors I think are really the ones that have to deal with this the most -- if something tells a shooter to enable and something else tells it to disable, who should win? At the moment, the way I deal with this is by guaranteed execution ordering (implicitly in teleopPeriodic and by order of location in class declaration), and so the 'last command wins'.

In your particular example of spit-in and spit-out of a ball -- if a single component is implementing it, then it can only be bringing a ball in, or spitting a ball out. There's no conflict here -- last command wins. I can't think of something at the moment that 'last command wins' wouldn't be able to solve.

### Conclusion

There's a lot in Magicbot that heavily depends on philosophy or convention to ensure that the user does things correctly. I'm open to changes that address those problems, and at the least I hope I will be able to update the documentation to reflect some of these deeper philosophies.

If you find more constructs that just don't work well in Magicbot, I'm interested to hear more about them. If you want me to review your magicbot port of your code, I might be able to find some time to do that too.

@uranusjr The issue isn't that pip upgrades. The problem is:

1. Django 1.8 starts getting installed.
2. VCS starts getting installed with dependencies.
   1. Pip checks whether a version of Django is installed that meets the VCS package's requirements (say `>1.7`)
   2. Django 1.8 isn't installed yet, so pip starts installing the newest version of Django possible for the VCS package's requirements (Django 1.11)
3. Django 1.8 finishes installing.
4. Django 1.11 finishes installing.

If I then re-run pipenv install I correctly end up with Django 1.8, or if I run `pipenv install --sequential`. It's not that pipenv is incorrectly upgrading Django 1.8, it's that due to parallel installation it doesn't even know that django is installed.

FWIW my preferred solution would be to install VCS repos without dependencies, like any other package.

Something I was _going_ to suggest as an alternative workflow that might address this is making it easy to pin to a specific version in the _Pipfile_ when installing.

I think it's slightly surprising but not completely unreasonable that pipenv interprets `foo = "*"` to mean "I just need to make sure _some_ version of foo is installed, the user doesn't care which". To that end, having something like `pipenv install --pin foo` which results in `foo = "==1.2.3"` instead of `foo = "*"` in the Pipfile (where 1.2.3 is the current latest version of foo) seems like it might help.

The issue with this though is that the behavior of a lot of packages can change a lot based on their dependencies (e.g., the same version of pylint can do totally different things depending on what version of astroid it's using), and packages don't pin their own deps exactly. So I don't think this actually gets anyone very far. :/

Just having a little debug, and it seems that the byte format may have changed.

For example, currently python-memcached is broken due to the use of ```get_module_constant("memcache", "__version__")``` in ```setup.py```

The line in question is (https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/setuptools/depends.py#L99)
```arg = bytes[ptr + 1] + bytes[ptr + 2] * 256 + extended_arg```

In python3.6 the line returns ```23040``` and the bytes object is ```array('b', [100, 0, 90, 0, 100, 1, ...])```  . This fails as is used to index the code.co_consts tuple that has 29 elements

In python3.5 the line returns ```0``` and the bytes object is ```array('b', [100, 0, 0, 90, 0, 0, 100, ...])``` and manages to index the code.co_consts tuple correctly.

Not sure where the underlying issue really lies, but it seems that the arg value is 8-bit in this particular use case in 3.6 and 16-bit in 3.5

This can be a tricky one to debug, but it's something we've seen as well.

One possibility is that this can arise if you're running Kafka in a container like docker or a vagrant VM. The problem is that it sees its hostname as the internal IP to the container, and then gives that to the client when it looks to connect to other brokers.  That is, instead of `localhost:9092` it might give `10.0.0.2:9092`, which may or may not be accessible to the client.  I can't tell based on the stacktrace if that's what's going on, but I know it's happened to us and was fixed by using the `advertised.host.name` Kafka setting so that it was an address which would be accessible.

The only other thing I can think of is to make sure you're up to date on Kafka. We've only extensively tested PyKafka with 0.8.2.1, so I can't guarantee it'll work with anything older.

I think the only way to truly debug this will be to set some logging or breakpoints so that it's clear what it's trying to connect to and being sure that's accessible.  There may also be some log messages in Kafka if the request is getting to the broker. Based on where this happens, that broker connection should already be established, so I'm not sure what's going on.

Looking at this I realize that you setting breakpoints in a client library isn't the most fun. I'm going to make a note to go through PyKafka and add a bunch of debug logging so it's more obvious what it's doing before crashes like this.

`chmod` could be used to open up all files for reading, but I'm not sure I'd call that a great solution. Even though the risk is minor, it discloses to all users that scikit-image is present. Not sure why that could be an issue, but I just don't feel right mucking about with permissions. It's also worth noting a lot of packages work this way, so it's not confined to our package.

However, if we wanted to be clever... try this on for size. Instead of `os.chmod`, we assume the owner of `setup.py` is the desired owner of the package. Introspecting that can be done with

``` python
import os
import pwd

username = pwd.getpwuid(os.stat('./setup.py').st_uid).pw_name

# After install complete, execute os.chown recursively ...
# or just execute the shell command with -R flag in a subprocess
```

Still not completely convinced it's anything but a solution searching for a problem, but if we want to do something about this I'd feel better "restoring ownership."

Also,  should I add a conversion via `img_as_float` for the input to `estimate_sigma`?  The estimate_sigma function will work fine for integer images (`PyWavelets` will convert to double precision internally in that case), but it appears the other denoising functions convert their inputs to float, which could potentially lead to users calling `estimate_sigma` on the integer image and then passing that sigma on to another function where it would be much too large once the image gets rescaled to [0, 1].

Right, thanks for the ping :-).

I spent a few hours looking at this last night, and it's complicated!

The easy part: there are a bunch of simpler, lower-level objects that model pickling will rely on. For the most part I think we just need to add some tests for these (pickle/unpickle, and ideally having some historical pickles stored in the test suite to test that new versions of patsy continue to be able to unpickle them):
* `EvalFactor`
* `LookupFactor`
* `Term` (and in particular making sure its `.name()` method continues to work)
* `EvalEnvironment`
* all the stateful transform classes (in `state.py`, `splines.py`, `mgcv_cubic_splines.py`) -- there's some shared testing code for these in `test_state.py:check_stateful` that could take care of a lot of the work
* `ContrastMatrix`

Then there's the core design stuff, mostly in `build.py`, which currently includes:
* the two `FactorEvaluator` classes
* `_ColumnBuilder`
* `DesignMatrixBuilder`

This is the hard part, because the internal representations here are a mess -- weird encoding of information, redundant encodings across different objects, and so on. This really needs some substantive refactoring.

And then there's a few high-level objects that need some attention:
* `DesignInfo` -- a little complicated because it has a few different possible states -- sometimes only partial metadata is available -- but I think probably fine except for tests
* `DesignMatrix` -- currently inherits `ndarray.__reduce__`, which causes it to lose the crucial `.design_info` attribute when going through a pickle/unpickle cycle, so that needs fixing

So I'm going to keep thinking about the best way to handle `build.py`, but at least this should give some picture of the landscape...

Suppose you have 2 portfolios with returns 90% and -80% after 60 periods. If you want to simulate the cumulative returns of 1 period you have to compute the rate of returns.

```
def one_period_returns_series(period, returns):
     daily_returns = (returns+1.)**(1./period) - 1
     return pd.Series([daily_returns] * period)

period = 60

s1 = one_period_returns_series(period, returns=0.90)
s2 = one_period_returns_series(period, returns=-0.80)
s1cr = s1.add(1).cumprod()
s2cr = s2.add(1).cumprod()

s1cr.name = 's1cr'
s2cr.name = 's2cr'
s1cr.plot(legend=True)
s2cr.plot(legend=True)
```

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/15273575/30751727-68b3411e-9fba-11e7-944d-7947afc774c4.png)

Then, if you like to plot the full portfolio cumulative returns you have 2 ways (avgcr1 and avgcr3):


```
# average the 1 period comulative return time series
avgcr1 = (s1cr+s2cr)/2 

# compute the final portfolio returns and build the 1 period rate of returns from that
s12_avg = (0.90-0.80) / 2.
avgcr3 = one_period_returns_series(period, s12_avg).add(1).cumprod()

avgcr2 = ((s1+s2)/2).add(1).cumprod() # this is completely wrong

avgcr1.name = 'avgcr1'
avgcr3.name = 'avgcr3'
s1cr.plot(legend=True)
s2cr.plot(legend=True)
avgcr1.plot(legend=True)
avgcr3.plot(legend=True)
```
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/15273575/30751873-fa8d8f18-9fba-11e7-88e8-9798641d1283.png)

They are slightly different, but both final values equal the value of the portfolio at the end of the 60 periods. The intermediate values are approximation so you might prefer one or the other.

Alphalens uses the avgcr3 approach and it applies it at every periods, using a rolling window of N-periods returns as input. Here is where the bug might be: compared to the example above, the N-portfolios are shifted by 1 period from each other so the cumulative returns build from that could be slightly wrong, I need to double check. The other approach, avgcr1,  is fine anyway.

Amend last commit with:
```bash
$ git commit --amend
```

Edit the commit message:
```
Multiple changes in CDN links

* Moving the resources from CDNJs to JSDelivr. In HTTP/2 and SPDY it is recommended to host all dependencies in the same CDN or web server, so all resources are downloaded using the same HTTP connection. It's faster than establish a connection to two CDN providers. In the past using multiple CDN was the best option, but no longer. So we have to put them in just one CDN, I don't have any relation with JSDelivr but think is the best available option.
* Added SRI (integrity) to external scripts. This prevents an infection if the CDN is compromised.
* Removed version aliasing from Algolia (3) and put specific version (3.13.1). This way the cache TTL is longer and avoid breaking changes if the library is updated.
* Added the JSDelivr RUM script. This allow them to test the performance of downloading scripts and takes better load balancing decision for the CDN resources.
```

then push force with:
```bash
$ git push -f
```

Hi @rsaftoiu! Thanks for the feedback and sorry I broke backwards compatibility for your use case.

During the pip 6.x upgrade pip-accel switched to using `pip install --download` which as you pointed out (unintentionally) broke backwards compatibility with previous versions of pip-accel. The reason for this is that when pip is given the `--download` option it internally enables `--ignore-installed`.

There is no documented way to avoid this behavior, however I just released pip-accel 0.26.3 which uses an undocumented way (via monkey patching) to restore backwards compatibility with the behavior of older versions of pip-accel. I'm not yet sure if I will regret this later on, but I take backwards compatibility fairly serious, which is why I decided to resort to using monkey patching to restore this behavior.

Please try out pip-accel 0.26.3 and let me know whether it resolves the issue you reported.

`pause: true` is stored in a `<cluster>/config` and works cluster-wide, but every node reads and applies it only on the next run of ha loop.
Basically we have only two options here:
 - since pause change request if always processed my the master we can "wake-up" the main HA loop and master will recognize change of pause state nearly immediately.

Both deployments happen from a docker container with the Serverless Framework.  The only real difference would be that when we run the docker container locally it's on a mac and on our gitlab runner it's a linux box.  Otherwise there really isn't much of a difference.

I've also tried following the exact steps locally on my mac as in our CI/CD pipeline and it still works correctly.  

Here is our dockerfile:

`FROM node

RUN apt-get update && DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive apt-get install -y \
  build-essential \
  python \
  python-dev \
  python-pip \
  curl \
  git-core && \
  rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/* && \
  apt-get clean

RUN pip install --upgrade --ignore-installed pip
RUN pip install virtualenv

RUN npm install serverless@1.20.2 -g
RUN npm install --prefix /sls_plugins serverless-python-requirements@2.5.0
RUN npm install --prefix /sls_plugins serverless-plugin-git-variables@2.0.0
RUN npm install --prefix /sls_plugins serverless-plugin-tracing@2.0.0
RUN npm install --prefix /sls_plugins serverless-prune-plugin@1.3.0
RUN npm install aws-sdk@2.101.0 -g

COPY .pydistutils.cfg /root/.pydistutils.cfg
COPY serverless_wrapper /usr/local/bin/serverless_wrapper
RUN chmod a+x /usr/local/bin/serverless_wrapper
COPY setup_ci_environment /usr/local/bin/setup_ci_environment
RUN chmod a+x /usr/local/bin/setup_ci_environment
COPY requirements_adfs.txt /root/requirements_adfs.txt
RUN mv /root/.pydistutils.cfg /root/.pydistutils.cfg_old && \
    pip install -r /root/requirements_adfs.txt && \
    rm -f /root/requirements_adfs.txt && \
    mv /root/.pydistutils.cfg_old /root/.pydistutils.cfg

COPY samlapi_adfs3.py /usr/local/bin/samlapi_adfs3.py
RUN chmod a+x /usr/local/bin/samlapi_adfs3.py

WORKDIR /workspace

CMD ["serverless"]
`

We pull this docker image to the machine and then run the setup ci script which is the following:
`
#!/bin/bash

mv /root/.pydistutils.cfg /root/.pydistutils.cfg_old
virtualenv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements-dev.txt --quiet
mv /root/.pydistutils.cfg_old /root/.pydistutils.cfg
ln -s -f /sls_plugins/node_modules ./.serverless_plugins
`

from google.appengine.ext import ndb

    _use_memcache = False
    b1 = ndb.BlobProperty(compressed=True)

    _use_memcache = False
    b1 = ndb.BlobProperty(compressed=True)
    b2 = ndb.BlobProperty(compressed=True, repeated=True)

I'm having strong feelings of "if it ain't broke, don't fix it" right now. It already does what most people say they think it should do.

@pcostell proposal is a hack to get the dictionary only approach working, but (correct me if I'm wrong Patrick) I don't think it's an endorsement of that approach.

There is no fundamental reason for dictionary only, other than making Python and JSON play happy together (which is not idiomatic Python FWIW).

The separation of data (via `__getitem__`), "address" (via `key`) and metadata (via `exclude_from_indexes`) is isomorphic to any other minimal formulation of the abstraction.

@tseaver Both.

I think it's best if I quickly also describe what I was doing.
The google.storage python api does not seem to have a better way to download a bunch of small files than to individually create a blob `blob = bucket.blob(name); blob.download_as_x()`, which is super slow.  In contrast, `gsutil -m cp x y` is really quick, but all metadata is lost.  
I needed that metadata as well.

So as a workaround I fetch all blobs I want to download with `gsutil ls -l`, create batches based on file size.  Each batch is then sent to a new process (using python `multiprocessing.Pool`) and each blob is downloaded (`blob.download_as_string()` and combine the dict with `blob.metadata`) in its own thread (using python `threading.Thread`).

When only multithreading I got no errors passing a single Client/Bucket (`storage.Client().get_bucket(name)`) to each thread.  
When I sent each batch to a new process and then multithreaded each blob in that batch I got the above errors.  Additionally, that whole batch would fail about 50% of the time, with subsequent batches usually succeeding to connect.  
Problem was solved creating and passing a new Bucket object for each process

Anyone interested in shading `google-cloud-datastore` so that it no longer presents problems in your own project just make a your own sub-module in your own repo with a pom like this:

```
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!--
  Because datastore-v1-proto-client v1.6.0 contains many out of date and clashing deps ...
-->
<project xmlns="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0" xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance"
         xsi:schemaLocation="http://maven.apache.org/POM/4.0.0 http://maven.apache.org/maven-v4_0_0.xsd">
    <modelVersion>4.0.0</modelVersion>
    <artifactId>shaded-datastore-v1-proto-client</artifactId>

    <name>Shaded version of ${project.parent.groupId}:${project.parent.artifactId}</name>

    <packaging>jar</packaging>

    <parent>
        <groupId>com.google.cloud.datastore</groupId>
        <artifactId>datastore-v1-proto-client-parent</artifactId>
        <version>1.6.0</version>
    </parent>

    <dependencies>

        <dependency>
            <groupId>com.google.cloud.datastore</groupId>
            <artifactId>datastore-v1-proto-client</artifactId>
            <version>${project.version}</version>
            <exclusions>
                <exclusion>
                    <groupId>com.google.guava</groupId>
                    <artifactId>guava-jdk5</artifactId>
                </exclusion>
            </exclusions>
        </dependency>

    </dependencies>

    <build>
        <plugins>

            <plugin>
                <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
                <artifactId>maven-gpg-plugin</artifactId>
                <version>1.6</version>
                <executions>
                    <execution>
                        <id>sign-artifacts</id>
                        <phase>deploy</phase>
                        <goals>
                            <goal>sign</goal>
                        </goals>
                    </execution>
                </executions>
            </plugin>


            <plugin>
                <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
                <artifactId>maven-shade-plugin</artifactId>
                <version>3.0.0</version>
                <executions>
                    <execution>
                        <phase>package</phase>
                        <goals>
                            <goal>shade</goal>
                        </goals>
                        <configuration>
                            <relocations>
                                <relocation>
                                    <pattern>com.google.common</pattern>
                                    <shadedPattern>googlecloud.shaded.com.google.common</shadedPattern>
                                </relocation>
                                <relocation>
                                    <pattern>org.codehaus.jackson</pattern>
                                    <shadedPattern>googlecloud.shaded.org.codehaus.jackson</shadedPattern>
                                </relocation>
                                <relocation>
                                    <pattern>com.fasterxml.jackson</pattern>
                                    <shadedPattern>googlecloud.shaded.com.fasterxml.jackson</shadedPattern>
                                </relocation>
                                <relocation>
                                    <pattern>org.apache.http</pattern>
                                    <shadedPattern>googlecloud.shaded.org.apache.http</shadedPattern>
                                </relocation>
                                <relocation>
                                    <pattern>org.apache.commons</pattern>
                                    <shadedPattern>googlecloud.shaded.org.apache.commons</shadedPattern>
                                </relocation>
                            </relocations>
                            <artifactSet>
                                <includes>
                                    <include>*:*</include>
                                </includes>
                                <excludes>
                                    <exclude>com.google.code.findbugs:jsr305</exclude>
                                </excludes>
                            </artifactSet>
                        </configuration>
                    </execution>
                </executions>
            </plugin>
        </plugins>
    </build>
</project>
```

Then elsewhere in your multi-module project depend on `com.google.cloud.datastore:shaded-datastore-v1-proto-client:1.6.0` instead of `com.google.cloud.datastore:datastore-v1-proto-client:1.6.0` and you are golden. Just don't publish this jar outside your own Nexus/Artifactory. No need to wait for #205 to be consumed and 1.6.1+to be released.

We can handle this `pyfastcopy` the same as the other c extension `cryptography` in the future, probably having different installers for different platforms as Ben mentioned before (Not sure). "Need" is a strong word! :) -- it works fine without it, but it could be good? And yeah, we'll need a writing function before it can be used for @lyw07's PR. Main question in terms of writing out a template is whether we leave the fields empty (and lean on the internally specified defaults, which has the advantage of adapting in case those defaults change across versions), or write the default values into the template file. A middle-ground could be writing a comment above the option line saying what the default is (but then this still won't get updated either when the software updates).

I was able to resolve the issue by increasing the number of files that can be open.

Bleach can drop disallowed tags (it's more than just unknown tags--it's all not-allowed tags) or it can escape them. So it can do `<x>y</x>` -> `y` as well as `<x>y</x>` -> `<x>y</x>`.

`<sarcasm/>` is an interesting input. I've seen it and similar things pop up occasionally. Bleach has always had issues with various categories of input, some of which I'd like to fix at some point. 

I have fears that it's indicative that there are other issues, too, since the pipeline will now be treating everything as if it were HTML and changing it rather than just HTML tags. I haven't finished the rewrite and there are still a lot of failing tests, but this was the first big issue I bumped into. My other fear is that the new architecture causes Bleach to take a step backwards rather than a step forwards and that it's now impossible to fix those things because the integral parts are inaccessible.

Seems like my theory on what's happening is either right on or really close.

What're our options? Should I subclass HTMLParser and override `_parse` so I can do sanitizing at the tokenizing step? Should I write my own treebuilder that deals with disallowed tags at that stage somehow? Maybe subclass HTMLParser and override `normalizeToken`?

Template commits are not directly supported in the `commit_all` method yet, but its something we're working on and should be available soon.  We'll use this issue to track progress.

In the meantime, you can use the `cmd=` argument to accomplish this with the raw commit XML.  For example:

To commit template 'abc':

    from pandevice import panorama
    pano = panorama.Panorama('10.0.0.5', 'admin', 'password')
    pano.commit_all(cmd="<commit-all><template><name>abc</name></template></commit-all>")

To commit devicegroup 'xyz' and include templates:

    from pandevice import panorama
    pano = panorama.Panorama('10.0.0.5', 'admin', 'password')
    pano.commit_all(cmd="<commit-all><shared-policy><include-template>yes</include-template><device-group><entry name='xyz'/></device-group></shared-policy></commit-all>")

> Is there a compelling reason to specify that the fileobj should have unbuffered IO?

Yes, for terminals, stdout is line-buffered, and stderr is unbuffered: We need to be able to read bytes waiting for input that have not yet sent a line-terminator, such as progress bars or screen-oriented programs using ncurses that never emit a line terminator.

I could be wrong, though.  This will require writing tests (and a sub-program that emits 1-byte at a time without flushing, to both stdout and stderr), and various assertions about reading single bytes, blocks of bytes, and lines.

Then, we could simply change the argument in ``io.open(fd, 'r+b', buffering=0)`` to -1 or so, and see what happens.  If it fails the tests, then the only thing we could do for you is allow you to specify your own ``buffering`` option, documenting the differences of behavior, and furthermore writing tests for such behavior.

* So far there is no reason to break backwards compatibility for the Python API.
* I introduced a dependency on python-dateutil in order to parse the string dates the Python API expects. This has the added advantage of allowing more date formats (dateutil will recognize a lot of different formats).
* `datetime` objects can now also be provided as timestamps (pretty much a side effect of parsing the date strings).
* If users want to avoid the performance overhead resulting from date string parsing and `datetime` handling, they can just pass in nanosecond integers.
* This is not implemented yet, but maybe we should use `warnings.warn` to warn people when they're specifying a database while using UDP. Selecting the database is now done on the server side for UDP and cannot be influenced by the client. Silently ignoring the database param might be confusing for users.

- Serializable now has attributes `rlp_` and `mutable_` (trailing underscore to distinguish from possibly clashing field names)
- if `mutable_` is `False`, attempts to set field values throw a `ValueError`. Note that (1) `mutable_` is not recursive (i.e. `block.mutable_` and `block.header.mutable_` are independent, although they have the same value after initialization) and (2) changes of existing objects are not tracked (e.g. it does not prevent appending to a list).
- `rlp.decode(s)` sets `rlp_` to `s` and (implicitly by `deserialize`) `mutable_` to `False`.
- `rlp.encode(obj)` returns `obj.rlp_` if it's there
- `rlp.encode(obj, sedes=SomeClass)` ignores `rlp_` (because different serializers can lead to different rlp, e.g. `rlp.encode(tx, Transaction)` vs `rlp.encode(tx, UnsignedTransaction)`)

To mutate an immutable object, set `mutable_` to `False` and (if the changes are persistent) `rlp_` to `None`.

Conda 4.4 maintains the behavior, with a default value for the configuration parameter `max_shlvl` of `2`.  Since this is a configuration parameter, it can certainly be set to `1`.

Conda 4.4 does implement environment stacking for the PATH environment variable (although not others, including PYTHONPATH).  That decision was made to maintain behavior unix users have had for at least the last couple years.  As a future enhancement, we should implement an alternate mode where environments are not stacked on PATH, and instead only one environment is active at a time, but we still have a CONDA_SHLVL stacking that can be pushed and popped.

Scratch that, I see that this is the desired behavior described above:
> The ideal situation is that, while a stack of active environments should be available to push onto and pop off of, only a single environment should be able to be active at any given time.

However, now that there's no stacking of PATHs, I don't quite see the value of maintaining this separate queue. I think there is something satisfying and intuitive about `conda activate env` being used to switch to a specific env, and `conda deactivate` actually *deactivating* conda (not just popping an env off the stack).

Could the default behavior in 4.6 to be equivalent to the old max_shlvl=1 behavior, instead?

I can see it being quite confusing for a user who may have switched back and forth between several environments while trying things out:

```
$ conda activate env1
(env1)$ conda activate env2
(env2)$ conda activate env3
(env3)$ conda activate env1
(env1)$ conda activate env2
(env2)$ conda activate env3
(env3)$ echo $CONDA_SHLVL
6
(env3)$ conda deactivate
(env2)$ conda deactivate
(env1)$ conda deactivate
(env3)$ conda deactivate
(env2)$ conda deactivate
(env1)$ conda deactivate
$
```
(There seems to be some special logic which treats a request to activate the most-recently active env as a pop, rather than a push, but it doesn't help in the general case).

Lastly, is there any way to inspect the stack?

Generally running sudo conda update x will work fine but its something I don't want to and shouldn't need to execute under sudo.

I have yet to find a way to reinstall just the conda executable. There's nothing broken with the python distribution, everything else still works, its just the conda command that fails to get rewritten after an update.

OS X.10, Macbook Pro, Mid 2011
I was able to fix it by adding the /home/xyz/install/anaconda3/bin to my PATH.

Unfortunately, I've done several reinstalls of Anaconda over time.  I **think** the most recent one was the command-line version, but I might be misremembering.  Is there something I can look at to figure out which installer was used most recently?

You can still use Python 2.7 with Anaconda3. Use conda to create a Python 2 environment, like

```
conda create -n py2 python=2 anaconda
activate py2
```

The important thing is that in Anaconda3, conda itself will be running in Python 3, so there won't be any issues (even if you use it to create a Python 2 environment).

Thanks for explaining your use case.  Obviously you have a valid reason to want to know when your config has changed.  I can think of other ways to accomplish this, but they all definitely involve writing more code in your application, as opposed to just setting a callback on the ``ConfigObj`` itself.  That suggests that ``on_change`` is probably a worthwhile feature in general.

With that being said, I'm still concerned about edge cases.  For example, let's say we pass the section instead of the section name.  At the point where the ``on_change`` callback is fired, it's possible for the section to have been instantiated but is not yet part of the parent ``ConfigObj`` object, e.g.

```python
>>> d = configobj.ConfigObj(on_change=lambda *args: print('bar', args))
>>> d['section'] = {'spam': 'eggs'}
bar ('section', 'spam', None, 'eggs')
bar (None, 'section', None, {'spam': 'eggs'})
```

This would normally be okay if we documented it, but because the section hasn't yet been attached to its parent, that means that you'll get an error when accessing certain fields.  For example, I tried changing the implementation to pass the section itself instead of the name, and I get this problem:

```python
>>> d = configobj.ConfigObj()
>>> d['sec'] = {'spam': 'eggs'}
>>> section = d['sec']
>>> section.parent  # section objects normally have an accessible parent
ConfigObj({'sec': {'spam': 'eggs'}})
>>>
>>>
>>> d = configobj.ConfigObj(on_change=lambda section, *args: print(section.parent))
>>> d['sec'] = {'spam': 'eggs'}
Traceback (most recent call last):
...  # traceback omitted for brevity
```

Normally I'd be okay with just documenting this limitation, but the whole point of wanting to pass in a section object rather than the name is that the callback might want to determine its place in the config hierarchy, which I can't see a way to do without inspecting the section parent.  Maybe we could pass another parameter indicating whether the section is still being initialized, though I'd hate to add a fifth required parameter, so perhaps an attribute on the section object itself would be better.  Either way, we'll probably need to figure out something clever.

If we did go forward with this, I agree that preventing the callback from being set after initialization is probably best, since it offers the least opportunities for weird edge cases.  The main problem is that it will stop people from waiting to set a callback until after they've loaded their values.  I tend to use chains of config files (e.g. having a ``development-defaults.ini`` file which is checked into my Git repo and a ``development.ini`` which is not checked in) which means that I instantiate a ``ConfigObj`` object and then call ``config.merge()`` for each file being merged together.  Because the callback fires for each individual value, that would result in literally thousands of calls.  Then again, maybe that's okay; in that situation someone could always write a callback with an if-statement that has it skip whatever it does until after a global variable is set indicating that your config has finished initializing.

These difficulties make me inclined to think that writing more code at the application level might be the way to go.  For example, you could wrap your ``ConfigObj`` object in another object which dispatches to it.  Here's a quick proof-of-concept I just whipped up:

```python
from __future__ import print_function
import configobj


class ChangeChecker(object):
    def __init__(self, conf, on_change):
        self.conf, self.on_change = conf, on_change

    def __getitem__(self, name):
        val = self.conf[name]
        if isinstance(val, dict):
            return ChangeChecker(val, self.on_change)
        else:
            return val

    def __setitem__(self, name, new_val):
        old_val = self.conf.get(name)
        self.conf[name] = new_val
        self.on_change(self.conf, name, old_val, new_val)


c = configobj.ConfigObj({'foo': 'bar'})
config = ChangeChecker(c, on_change=lambda *args: print('on_change', *args))
config['foo'] = 'baz'
config['sec'] = {'spam': 'eggs', 'knights': 'nee'}
config['sec']['hello'] = 'world'
```

The final three lines produce the following output:

```
on_change {'foo': 'baz'} foo bar baz
on_change {'foo': 'baz', 'sec': {'knights': 'nee', 'spam': 'eggs'}} sec None {'knights': 'nee', 'spam': 'eggs'}
on_change {'knights': 'nee', 'spam': 'eggs', 'hello': 'world'} hello None world
```

One of the big advantages (from my perspective) of doing it this way is that when you set an entire section, it only fires the callback once, which is something that would be rather difficult to make happen of we bundle this into ConfigObj without a major rewrite of our current approach.

Anyway, I'm still not totally against including this feature, and I do appreciate anyone who actually opens a pull request!  We'd just need to figure out some ways around the difficulties, so having examined the issue a little more in depth I'm curious what your thoughts are on the viability of this whole thing, vs something like the wrapper object approach I suggested above.

Hi @reeshabhraj1, I was able to reproduce your test case with the extra info provided by the support team. We indeed do not validate the consistency between content-length and content-range, because that is guaranteed by the service. 

Another thing I wanted to clarify is: when the SDK ask the service for a range of data, and that range is valid, then the service will always provide the entirety of that data. For example, your blob has 171490 bytes; if we ask the service for the range 0-171489, then the service always returns 171490 bytes. If the connection breaks for some reason in the middle, the call will be retried.

I'm a bit curious about why you are simulating this failure from the Storage service, could you please provide some insights?

Ah yes this makes sense based on how the key for a repository is configured.

It currently creates one git repository for each `(repo, ref)` pair and then validates install state inside that cloned repository.  A workaround would be to use a *slightly* different repository name or ref.

There's some [recently added code](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/7139a47c1ca968a2699e467279677fa77ad68aae/pre_commit/store.py#L133-L139) for local repositories that can probably be borrowed here such that different `additional_dependencies` repositories get their own directories.   Notice that here, the key becomes `('local:sorted,joined,deps', 'N/A')` -- perhaps something similar can be done for normal repositories as well!

An aside: pylint is a bit of a beast to get working with pre-commit as most of its checks aren't static analysis but *dynamic* analysis (it needs to be able to import your code to check it).  pylint often works better as a `local` / `system` / `script` hook which takes advantage of the currently activated virtualenv (and then has the ability to import and inspect your code directly).

Here's the issue to follow as well: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/issues/191

I think it's probably not worth adding special code to every single hook which acts on files?  We've been working around this by adding:

```yaml
exclude: ^(submodule/path|symlink/path)$
```

to these specific hooks.

The test files are partitioned like the directories in the python wrapper. But yes, with the intent of being long files instead of directories (it's an ethos shared by other nodejs projects). Admittedly I have fewer tests than the python wrapper. My goal was to simply assert the function binding exists and does the json dance. For example, many of the build-a-request functions I don't submit the transaction b/c I'm just testing that the binding exists and returns a json object for the request. The cpp and js code for this wrapper is very thin and repetitive.

The test runner, [ava](https://www.npmjs.com/package/ava), runs each test concurrently. Every test creates its own pool/wallet(s) so tests are atomic. Yes, right now every file has a single test that calls several indy functions and is stateful :astonished: but it’s really not that bad IMHO when the api changes you either change a bunch of small tests or you change one long test. Either way there is work and debugging involved.

Yes, I plan on maintaining it. ...assuming I don’t get hit by a bus :)
And as time goes on, we can always add more tests.

Hi,

This is not a pytest-asyncio specific issue actually: pure `xUnit` test classes (`TestCase` subclasses) can't access pytest fixtures, because tests can't have any other parameter besides `self` in the `xUnit` framework.

You will have to convert the class to "pytest-style" test class instead:

```python
class Test:  # note that no subclassing is needed

    @pytest.mark.asyncio
    def test_normalize_button_value(self, event_loop):
        my_serial = EsploraSerial("/dev/ttyACM0", self.q, self.cond)
        resp = event_loop.run_until_complete(my_serial.normalize_button_value(self.list_test_data, 1))
        assert resp == '0'
```

So which improvements specifically are you proposing?

* Improving the default event_loop fixture to return a ProactorEventLoop on Windows?
* Improving our child watcher support?
* Improving our finalizer to check if the old loop is already closed?

With sufficient rationale, I'd be open to merging in all of these :)

@Tinche rationale is in the readme, it's literally a bunch of features allowing more flexibility.

> So, `accept_global_loop` should default to True
Unfortunately, this leads to very weird errors for a library developer who decided to use a custom user-provided event loop instead of always getting the global one due to programming errors when the loop wasn't explicitly passed to `asyncio` functions, like `sleep`.

The rest is okay to me.

Did you consider to just leave these commands in a `.madx` file and `madx.call()` that file from cpymad? This is much easier and recommended if you are only going to change parameters manually. In general, you probably don't need cpymad if you are not trying to write a program which generates commands automatically or adjusts parameters dynamically.

If you really do need python code to input these commands: Most MAD-X commands can be converted to python syntax pretty straight forward, e.g.:

```python
# (assuming your Madx instance is called `madx`:)
madx.command.select(flag="error", clear=True)
# note the trailing underscore in 'class_' is needed because class is a python keyword:
madx.command.select(flag="error", class_="quadrupole")
# Now this command is a bit non-standard and currently cpymad does a bad job getting it right.
# But you can still generate your command string manually and input it to cpymad:
madx.input('efcomp, order:=0, radius:=0.013, dknr={tgauss(2)*1e-4,tgauss(2)*1e-3};')
madx.command.esave(file="efield.tab0")
```

I agree that many of this should be simplified and made more straight forward, but this is not on the top of my priority list right now;)

@johnyquest7 - kaggle kernel is not working at all for me. However since you're only hitting this issue on kaggle kernel, I believe I know what is going on.

turicreate needs a location on disk to write temporary files. I believe the default for that location is not writable in kaggle kernel. When I set my location to an unwritable path, I get the exact same behavior you're seeing on kaggle kernel.

Please try doing the following right after you have imported turicreate:
`tc.config.set_runtime_config('TURI_CACHE_FILE_LOCATIONS', '<writable-path>')`
Where `<writable-path>` is a path where you have write permissions. Either relative or absolute paths work here.

Please give that a try and let us know if it works. If it does work, let's keep this issue open till we have a better error message in place.

> The error point out can't put variant shape to tensor4.

By definition, a tensor has a fixed size in each dimension. So you cannot combine images of different sizes in a single mini-batch. You will either need to group images of the same size into separate mini-batches (e.g., have a mini-batch with only 48x48 images, and another one with only 52x45 images), or employ some kind of masking (e.g., remember which part of the mini-batch tensor contains valid input and make sure only that part participates in the loss function -- this is easy with global pooling in the end, but hard with spatial pyramid pooling as you'll end up having to pool each image on its own).

The `result` that `path` returns is good information from a testing/debugging standpoint, giving the source type, target type, and function to be called for each conversion hop. That itself could be returned to the user to inspect or interact with. We could also go a step further and include some test utils that allow a user to pass a function and kwargs in and assert that the function passed is present in `result` and that it would be called with the given kwargs, or to call various `inspect` functions to show the module or code that would get run for debugging purposes. There could also be a test function to express more cleanly if a path exists at all rather than just letting NetworkX raise `networkx.NetworkXNoPath`.

I am having this same issue (Linux Mint 18.3). 

My player is already set to mpv and I can successfully play the downloaded song from the command line--I just can't play in mps-youtube.

On my system I will always get pretty current position. However, Dbus is quite slow. I did some benchmarks and it takes avg. 0.0024 sec (on my system) to execute Get on position. But this gap is probably not what you had on mind.

I suppose plugin just periodicaly executes all those calls in mpris2, right? Then what is the rate (probaly main cause of gap)? Also all calls in that file are synchronous, so it will take even more to get position update. How expensive are they? First there is a `ListNames` (bus enumeration) call to dbus itself. It returns all names on bus, which may be a lot. Just check

```sh
dbus-send --session                     \        
  --dest=org.freedesktop.DBus \
  --type=method_call                   \
  --print-reply=literal                   \
  /org/freedesktop/DBus              \
  org.freedesktop.DBus.ListNames |
tr ' ' '\n | grep -v "^$"
```
If it finds a mpris bus in that it then it executes `Get` for Metadata, PlaybackStatus and Position. Those 3 calls are nothing compared to bus enumeration.

`ListNames` could certainely be called only once on init and then only when bus it has been using disappears. This btw also deals with some race conditions when running multiple mpris players.

Another thing that should be called only once is suprisingly call to Position. Mpris is designed such that you get `Position` only once and then updates position only on your side by Rate (see Rationale for Rate property). When Rate changes you will get signal on property change. When player seeked you will get Seeked signal. This approach is strongly recomended by specs. (and only reasonable way to do it as dbus is clumsy and you dont want to mess it with many calls)

Probably the proper way to do that: just enumerate bus once, subscribe to signals for Rate, Seeked, Metadata, PlaybackStatus (yes those properties also emit signals on change). This approach requires main loop.

With @lousapetr we have looked more deeply into this issue and we found out that something bizarre is happening.
The problem can be traced further to the `_get_metadata_from_lastfm()` function in `util`, particularly to the line with `json.loads()` call.

Testing with `https://youtube.com/watch?v=V1O3a-ccLaA` we enabled the debug_mode in `g.py` and inserted prints to `_get_metadata_from_lastfm()` and modified the  input of `json.loads()` to string (default was 'bytes' (b'string') ):
```
#print( json.loads(resp.read()))    #this simply fails to play without any error
print( json.loads(resp.read().decode('utf-8')))
data = json.loads(resp.read().decode('utf-8'))
#import requests         #fix
#data = requests.get(url).json()
print('TEST')
```
Weirdly this prints `data` but not the 'TEST' string.

```

    metadata = util._get_metadata(song.title)
