As far as I know ```ganga``` registers the shutdown hooks correctly. The difficulty is when to run them in the unittests and to deal with the bringing up/shutting down of any Ganga services with this.

I don't think we can hope to have a ```setUp``` which looks just like ```from ganga import *``` I think we'll still need to have some logic to do things like re-generate repositories when the location changes and things like this.
It would be nice to see more of the code from ```GangaUnitTest``` put into simple helper functions.

I think from the perspective of tests we need:
 0. Load the Config (if required)
 1. Bootstrap ganga (still required for the moment from the Config system).
 2. Initialize the Registries
 3. Start any Ganga services demanded by the Config. (Monitoring, Tasks, Queues....)

At the end of each test (as in shutDownClass) we at least need to:
 1. Shutdown Ganga services currently running
 2. Shutdown the Registries (This takes care of the lockfile monitoring as well)
 3. Clear the Registry and on-disk elements we've modified.

TODO (feel free to correct me but I think these items need addressing, although probably not in order):

 1. Move delattr of Ganga.GPI Objects into shutdown methods to make sure the GPI after a shutdown is really clean. This may require care with the credentials system as it is.
 2. Separate tearDown from tearDownClass functions which can be tricky but allows us to only shutdown the registries once per test which is a bit more sane than the current system.
 3. Take common code from GangaUnitTest and bootstrap and put them into methods which are common to both and integrate these into 'ganga'.
 4. Separate loading of the config/plugins from starting of the registries and populating of the GPI objects.
 5. Possible cleanup of ```ganga/__init__.py``` after everything is working so we have a single function to load the config, then call the functions to load the GPI objects and then call the function to start the services.

Now, I see that your codes "says" it will only do 1 "bad" request, but you'll also have to take into account scrapy retries, crawlera retries, and the session proxy being banned after hitting a banned site. So this isn't controllable by Scrapy, but how Crawlera works.

Now, Scrapy retries can be configured with [`RETRY_TIMES`] (https://doc.scrapy.org/en/latest/topics/downloader-middleware.html#std:setting-RETRY_TIMES) and Crawlera retries with the [`X-Crawlera-Max-Retries`](https://doc.scrapinghub.com/crawlera.html#x-crawlera-max-retries) header.

I think the counter is correct, it is taken into account so the spider can finish because it is doing bad requests to a domain, which not only affects your spider and the data it gets, but also the site because of unnecessary bad requests.

The main reasons we went with VB instead of Docker for the codelabs is because Docker doesn't support [USB passthrough](https://docs.docker.com/docker-for-mac/faqs/#can-i-pass-through-a-usb-device-to-a-container). While that's not a big deal for the simulation codelab, USB passthrough is needed for codelabs involving real Thread devices. Docker recommends using the legacy [Docker Toolbox](https://docs.docker.com/toolbox/overview/#whats-in-the-box) if one needs USB passthrough, which uses VB under the covers. So we decided to go with straight VirtualBox to reduce complexity for codelab users.

That said, I'm looking forward to seeing Docker added once you figure out the networking bit!

Edouard.

My preference is to take the most conservative route and only explicitly allow defaultdict to compare to dict if there is a compelling need.

Even allowing `collections.defaultdict` could require special case handling. The comparison code can cause new default keys to be inserted into the defaultdict.  Assertions should not have side effects (OTOH the entire point of defaultdict is to be a side effect, no idea what to make of that).  A defaultdict would need to be converted into a plain dict for purposes of comparison without side effects.

Wait until someone has a need for dict ordering to be part of the comparison and decide what to do about 3.7 dict and OrderedDict at that time.  That one may require a mode flag or new method such as your strict_type_check suggestion.  For now people think of dicts as always unordered, but that is likely to change over time thanks to 3.7's behavior.

That there are special cases needed for these already suggests that the strict type check is a good thing.  Not being strict in test comparisons can lead to unintentionally passing tests, which is worse than an unintentionally failing tests as it isn't likely to be detected.
